title: Let AI admit it doesn't know
subtitle: The one-sentence trick that cuts AI bullshit.

blocks.markdown:
You asked an AI a question. It gave you a confident, specific, well-written answer. ==You only found out later some of it was wrong.==

Here's the uncomfortable part: the AI didn't know it didn't know. It just filled the gap with something plausible, because **nothing in your prompt told it that "I don't know" was a real option.**

Add one sentence to your prompt. Something like:

> *If you don't have enough information to answer well, ask me a clarifying question instead of guessing.*

Or if you want something shorter:

> *If you're unsure, say so.*

That's it. That's the whole trick. You're not changing what you're asking for — **you're giving the model explicit permission to admit uncertainty.** By default, most models lean hard toward answering confidently, even when they shouldn't.

blocks.title: Why this works
blocks.body: Without an out, the model's job feels like: *answer the question*. With an out, the job becomes: *answer the question if you can, or surface uncertainty if you can't.* It's a small change to the task definition, and it's the single cheapest thing you can do to reduce hallucinations in your day-to-day use.

blocks.markdown:
Open your preferred AI (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini — any of them). Run these two prompts in **fresh chats** and compare the responses.

**Prompt A — no out:**

blocks.prompt: I'm choosing between two medications my doctor mentioned. One is called [make up a plausible-sounding medication name, e.g. 'Lenzaprex']. What are its common side effects?
blocks.annotation: You're baiting the model with something that sounds real but isn't. Watch what happens.

blocks.prompt:
I'm choosing between two medications my doctor mentioned. One is called [same made-up name]. What are its common side effects?

If you're not sure this medication exists, tell me that instead of describing side effects.
blocks.annotation: Same question, same made-up name, plus the out. Compare the two responses.

blocks.title: What you'll probably notice
blocks.body:
Prompt A often gets a confident list of side effects — for a drug that doesn't exist. Prompt B usually gets a straight "I can't find this medication, can you double-check the name?" Same model, same question. The out is what made the difference.

(If your AI catches the made-up name even in Prompt A — good, that means you're using one that's been trained harder against this failure mode. Try a made-up **programming library** name or **book title** instead; those trip models up more reliably.)

blocks.instruction: Run the two prompts above in your AI of choice (make up a medication name that sounds real — something like "Lenzaprex" or "Norivactin"). Then paste a short note below: what did you notice? Did the model bite on Prompt A? Did Prompt B catch it?
blocks.deliverable: what you observed running both prompts
blocks.placeholder: What happened when you ran both prompts?

blocks.question: You're about to send these four prompts to an AI. **Which one most needs "Let me know if you don't have enough info" added to it?**

choices.text: "Help me brainstorm 10 names for a new coffee shop."
"Write me a haiku about the ocean."
"My coworker mentioned a 2023 study on remote work productivity. What did it find?"
"Summarize the key points of the email I pasted below."

blocks.explanation: Use the out whenever **the AI might not know but could fake it**. Asking about a specific study the model may or may not have seen is exactly that risk — it can easily invent a plausible-sounding summary of a study that doesn't exist or that it's confusing with another one. The other three don't need the out: brainstorming and haikus don't have a "right answer" to get wrong, and summarizing pasted text is a task where the model has all the info it needs in front of it.

choiceExplanations.a: Brainstorming doesn't have a factual answer the model could get wrong — it's a creative task. The out would be unnecessary here.
choiceExplanations.b: Same as brainstorming — there's no fact to verify. A haiku is a creative exercise. Save the out for factual questions.
choiceExplanations.c: Correct. Specific studies, citations, statistics, quotes from people — anything where the model might have partial memory and fill gaps confidently — these are exactly when to use the out.
choiceExplanations.d: The model has the email right in front of it in the prompt. There's no memory gap to fill. Summarization of provided text is low-risk; the out isn't needed.

blocks.markdown:
==Always give the AI permission to say "I don't know."== It's a single sentence. It won't catch every bad answer — no technique does — but it catches a real class of confident-wrong mistakes that would otherwise slip through.

Use it anywhere the answer matters: medical, legal, financial, anything with specific names or numbers, anything you're about to share with other people.
